---
title: "Installation"
---

## Prerequisites {#sec-installation}

ferx requires R ≥ 4.2, Rust (stable toolchain), and cmake.
Enzyme (LLVM) is optional — it enables exact automatic differentiation for
faster gradients, but is not needed to get started.

| Dependency | Required | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| R ≥ 4.2 | Yes | Package interface |
| Rust / Cargo | Yes | Compile the ferx-core backend; stable toolchain is sufficient |
| cmake | Yes | Used by the Rust build (Linux/macOS; bundled with Rtools44 on Windows) |
| Enzyme (LLVM) | No | Faster gradients via autodiff; documented in @sec-enzyme |

Two installation paths are described below:

- **Quick install** (@sec-quick-install) — stable Rust + finite-difference
  gradients. Works on all platforms. Identical estimates to the autodiff
  path, slightly slower. **Start here.**
- **Full Enzyme install** (@sec-enzyme) — a custom Rust fork with the
  Enzyme autodiff plugin. Recommended once you start fitting larger models or
  using AD-only features.

### Two separate one-time costs {#sec-two-costs}

The "full install takes hours" reputation conflates **two distinct,
one-time** steps. Knowing which you are paying for avoids confusion:

| Step | What it is | Roughly | When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Build the Enzyme toolchain** | Compile a custom nightly `rustc` + LLVM with Enzyme from source (@sec-enzyme) | ~45–60 min | **Once per machine.** The toolchain is reused forever (lives at `~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain`). *Autodiff path only.* |
| **2. Compile ferx** | Build the `ferx` / `ferx-core` Rust crates and link them | ~1–2 h (autodiff) / faster (FD) | **First install only.** Cargo caches dependencies, so later reinstalls/updates take minutes. |

So:

- **First-time autodiff user** pays both → ~2–3 h end-to-end.
- **First-time finite-difference user** pays only a faster version of step 2
  (no step 1; the FD build uses thin LTO and links noticeably faster).
- **Returning user** pays neither — both are cached.

Step 2 is slower on the autodiff path specifically because it uses **fat
LTO**, which is required for cross-crate Enzyme correctness — see the note in
@sec-enzyme.

## Quick install by platform {#sec-quick-install}

### macOS {#sec-macos}

cmake is the only non-standard system dependency. A stable Rust toolchain
is sufficient.

**Step 1 — Install Homebrew** (skip if already installed)

```bash
/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"
```

**Step 2 — Install cmake**

```bash
brew install cmake
```

**Step 3 — Install Rust**

```bash
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
```

Follow the on-screen instructions to add Rust to your PATH (usually
`source "$HOME/.cargo/env"`, or restart the terminal).

**Step 4 — Install ferx from R**

RStudio on macOS launches with a restricted PATH that does not include the
Homebrew cmake bin directory, even when the shell profile is configured
correctly. Add it explicitly before installing:

```r
# Apple Silicon (M1/M2/M3):
Sys.setenv(PATH = paste("/opt/homebrew/opt/cmake/bin", Sys.getenv("PATH"), sep = ":"))
# Intel Mac — use this line instead:
# Sys.setenv(PATH = paste("/usr/local/opt/cmake/bin", Sys.getenv("PATH"), sep = ":"))

Sys.setenv(FERX_NO_AUTODIFF = "1")
pak::pak("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
# or: devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
```

Without the `Sys.setenv(PATH = ...)` line, the Rust build fails with a
`cmake not found` error when launched from RStudio.

### Windows {#sec-windows}

Native Windows installs are supported with finite-difference gradients.
The `ferx-r` build auto-detects the missing Enzyme toolchain and falls back
automatically — no `FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1` flag is needed on Windows. All
estimation methods work; AD-only features are unavailable.

Native Enzyme autodiff is **not** available on Windows because the custom
Rust fork does not currently build there. For autodiff on Windows, use the
Docker image (@sec-docker), WSL2 + Ubuntu, or a remote Linux host.

**Step 1 — Install Rtools44**

[Rtools44](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/rtools44/rtools.html)
ships cmake and the MinGW gcc that R uses to link the package, so no
separate cmake install is needed.

**Step 2 — Install rustup with the GNU-ABI toolchain**

Download from <https://www.rust-lang.org/tools/install>, then in PowerShell:

```powershell
rustup toolchain install stable-x86_64-pc-windows-gnu
```

You do **not** need to `rustup default` it — the package's build pins this
toolchain automatically on Windows. (The rustup default on Windows is the
MSVC ABI, which is not link-compatible with Rtools' MinGW linker.)

**Step 3 — Install ferx from R**

```r
pak::pak("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
# or: devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
```

First install takes ~1–2 hours (Rust compile from scratch); subsequent
installs are fast.

### Linux {#sec-linux}

Install cmake and Rust via your system package manager plus rustup.
RStudio on Linux inherits the system PATH, so no PATH workaround is needed.

```bash
# Debian / Ubuntu
sudo apt-get install -y cmake build-essential curl git
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh
source "$HOME/.cargo/env"
```

```r
Sys.setenv(FERX_NO_AUTODIFF = "1")
pak::pak("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
# or: devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
```

## Verify the installation

```r
library(ferx)
ferx_example()          # lists all bundled examples
ex <- ferx_example("warfarin")
fit <- ferx_fit(ex$model, ex$data)
print(fit)
```

The first install compiles the Rust backend, which takes ~1–2 hours.
Subsequent installs reuse Cargo's artifact cache and are much faster.

## The FERX_NO_AUTODIFF flag {#sec-no-autodiff}

Setting `FERX_NO_AUTODIFF = "1"` skips the Enzyme autodiff toolchain probe
and forces **finite-difference gradients**. FD is slightly slower but
produces identical estimates. LLVM/Enzyme are not needed. All examples in
this book run with `FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1` on CI.

The flag is only required when you want to skip the probe in scripts or
CI; the build will automatically fall back to FD when no Enzyme toolchain
is found.

## Advanced: full Enzyme install {#sec-enzyme}

Enzyme unlocks exact gradients via automatic differentiation of the Rust
source, and is required for a few AD-only features (e.g. SAEM HMC
proposals via `n_leapfrog`). The toolchain is a custom fork of nightly
Rust built from source.

- **Total time:** ~45–60 min toolchain build + ~1–2 h first `ferx-r` install.
- **One-time setup.** Once steps 1–4 are done, the Enzyme toolchain lives
  at `~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain` and survives reboots and `/tmp`
  purges. To reinstall or update ferx-r later, you only need step 5.

::: {.callout-note}
## The first autodiff compile is slow — and the end *looks* like a hang

The autodiff build uses **fat LTO** (`lto = "fat"`), which is *required* for
cross-crate Enzyme correctness: the `ferx-core` functions being
differentiated are inlined into the final `ferx` crate so Enzyme can see
through the call boundary. Fat LTO re-optimizes the whole dependency graph
in one pass, so the **final crate (`Compiling ferx …`) routinely takes many
minutes** with no further per-crate progress output — this is normal, not a
stall. Confirm it is working with `ps aux | grep rustc` (you'll see one busy
`rustc --crate-name ferx` process).

This is distinct from the *quick install* path (@sec-quick-install), which
uses **thin LTO** and links noticeably faster. Only the **first** autodiff
compile pays the full cost; Cargo caches dependencies, so later reinstalls
are fast.

If `rustc` instead pins a core at 100% CPU *during the autodiff verification
step* (@sec-enzyme, Step 3) and never returns, that is a different problem —
an LLVM/Enzyme mismatch — covered there.
:::

::: {.panel-tabset}

### macOS

#### Step 1. Build dependencies

```bash
xcode-select --install                       # clang, git (skip if already installed)
brew install cmake ninja python3             # needed by the rust build system

curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh -s -- -y
source "$HOME/.cargo/env"
rustup toolchain install nightly --profile minimal
```

#### Step 2. Build the Enzyme-enabled Rust toolchain from source (~45–60 min)

```bash
git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/rust-lang/rust /tmp/rust-src
cd /tmp/rust-src

# IMPORTANT: --set llvm.download-ci-llvm=false avoids the LLVM/Enzyme mismatch
# that causes rustc to hang silently when compiling autodiff code.
./configure \
    --release-channel=nightly \
    --enable-llvm-enzyme \
    --enable-llvm-link-shared \
    --enable-ninja \
    --disable-docs \
    --set llvm.download-ci-llvm=false

./x build --stage 1 library                  # the long step (~45–60 min)

# Register the freshly built toolchain with rustup under the name "enzyme"
rustup toolchain link enzyme build/host/stage1
rustc +enzyme --version --verbose            # sanity check: prints version info
```

#### Step 3. Verify autodiff works (fat LTO is mandatory for Enzyme)

```bash
cat > /tmp/ad_check.rs <<'EOF'
#![feature(autodiff)]
use std::autodiff::autodiff_forward;

#[autodiff_forward(df, Dual, Const, Dual)]
#[inline(never)]
fn f(x: f64, y: f64) -> f64 { (x * y).exp() + x * x }

fn main() {
    let (val, dfdx) = df(1.5, 1.0, 2.0);
    let expect = 2.0 * (1.5 * 2.0_f64).exp() + 2.0 * 1.5;
    assert!((dfdx - expect).abs() < 1e-9);
    println!("autodiff OK: f={val:.4}, df/dx={dfdx:.4}");
}
EOF

rustc +enzyme -Zautodiff=Enable -Clto=fat -Copt-level=2 /tmp/ad_check.rs -o /tmp/ad_check
/tmp/ad_check
```

Expected: compiles in seconds, prints `autodiff OK: f=22.3355, df/dx=43.1711`.

::: {.callout-warning}
If `rustc` hangs at low/constant memory for many minutes (RSS stays under
~100 MB and never grows), the LLVM/Enzyme link is broken. Rebuild Step 2,
making sure `--set llvm.download-ci-llvm=false` is set.
:::

#### Step 4. Move the toolchain to a permanent location (macOS purges `/tmp`)

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.local/share

# -L dereferences the LLVM/Enzyme dylib symlinks into real files, so the copy
# still works after /tmp/rust-src is deleted. Cycle warnings are harmless.
cp -aL /tmp/rust-src/build/host/stage1 ~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain

# Prune the bundled rust source tree (only used for IDE source browsing).
# Without this the copy is ~14-30 GB; after pruning it is ~640 MB.
rm -rf ~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain/lib/rustlib/rustc-src \
       ~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain/lib/rustlib/src

du -sh ~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain        # should be ~640 MB

# Re-point the rustup "enzyme" toolchain at the permanent path
rustup toolchain uninstall enzyme
rustup toolchain link enzyme ~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain

# Re-verify against the new path
rustc +enzyme -Zautodiff=Enable -Clto=fat -Copt-level=2 /tmp/ad_check.rs -o /tmp/ad_check && /tmp/ad_check

# Reclaim ~8 GB now that the permanent copy is verified
rm -rf /tmp/rust-src
```

#### Step 5. Install ferx-r (in R, ~1–2 h first time, fast thereafter)

```r
pak::pak("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
# or: devtools::install_github("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
As long as `/tmp/rust-src` is gone but `~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain`
survives, you only ever need to repeat **Step 5** to reinstall or update
ferx-r.
:::

### Linux

Tested on Ubuntu 22.04 / 24.04. Other distributions work with
package-manager adjustments (substitute `apt` for `dnf`, `pacman`, etc.).

#### Step 1. Build dependencies and rustup

```bash
# Remove snap rustup if present (it conflicts with the rustup.rs installer):
sudo snap remove rustup 2>/dev/null || true

# Install build dependencies:
sudo apt update
sudo apt install -y cmake ninja-build clang g++ python3 \
                    build-essential curl git pkg-config libssl-dev libzstd-dev

# Install rustup and nightly:
curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh -s -- -y
source "$HOME/.cargo/env"
rustup toolchain install nightly --profile minimal
```

#### Step 2. Build the Enzyme-enabled Rust toolchain from source (~45–60 min)

```bash
git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/rust-lang/rust /tmp/rust-src
cd /tmp/rust-src

./configure \
    --release-channel=nightly \
    --enable-llvm-enzyme \
    --enable-llvm-link-shared \
    --enable-ninja \
    --disable-docs \
    --set llvm.download-ci-llvm=false

./x build --stage 1 library

rustup toolchain link enzyme build/host/stage1
rustc +enzyme --version --verbose
```

#### Step 3. Verify autodiff works

Same as the macOS Step 3 above — compile and run `/tmp/ad_check.rs` with
`rustc +enzyme -Zautodiff=Enable -Clto=fat -Copt-level=2`.

#### Step 4. Move the toolchain to a permanent location

`/tmp` is cleared on reboot on most Linux distros (and by
`systemd-tmpfiles` periodic cleanup on Ubuntu), which would silently
break the toolchain link.

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.local/share
cp -aL /tmp/rust-src/build/host/stage1 ~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain
rm -rf ~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain/lib/rustlib/rustc-src \
       ~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain/lib/rustlib/src
du -sh ~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain        # should be ~640 MB

rustup toolchain uninstall enzyme
rustup toolchain link enzyme ~/.local/share/enzyme-toolchain

rm -rf /tmp/rust-src
```

For multi-user / shared-host installs at `/opt/rust-nightly`, see
[`INSTALL-SYSADMIN.md`](https://github.com/FeRx-NLME/ferx-r/blob/main/INSTALL-SYSADMIN.md)
in the ferx-r repository.

#### Step 5. Install ferx-r

```r
pak::pak("FeRx-NLME/ferx-r")
```

### Windows

The Enzyme-enabled Rust fork does not currently build on native Windows.
Use one of:

- **WSL2 + Ubuntu** — `wsl --install -d Ubuntu`, then follow the Linux
  workflow inside the Ubuntu shell.
- **Docker image** (@sec-docker) — bundles a pre-built Enzyme toolchain.
- **Remote Linux host** — SSH into a Linux server or cloud VM.

:::

If Enzyme is available the install log will contain:

```
[build] autodiff: Enzyme backend enabled
```

## Docker / reproducible environments {#sec-docker}

The `ferx-r` repo ships a `Dockerfile` that bundles the Enzyme toolchain
(built from source), the `ferx` CLI, the `ferx` R package, and RStudio
Server — no local Rust/Enzyme setup required. On Windows this is the
recommended path if you need Enzyme autodiff.

```bash
git clone https://github.com/FeRx-NLME/ferx-r
cd ferx-r

# Build (first build takes ~45–60 min; cached after that)
docker build -t ferx:latest .

# Run RStudio Server
docker run --rm -p 8787:8787 -e PASSWORD=ferx ferx:latest
# -> open http://localhost:8787   user: rstudio   password: ferx
```

For CI without Enzyme, a minimal image is sufficient:

```dockerfile
FROM rocker/r-ver:4.4.0
ENV FERX_NO_AUTODIFF=1
RUN Rscript -e "pak::pak('FeRx-NLME/ferx-r')"
```
